clinicalStem: A 9-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department with a 2-week history of left ear pain and discharge that partially improved with oral amoxicillin. Over the past 48 hours his mother reports worsening headache, high fever (39.8°C), and new onset of drowsiness. On examination he is confused, has neck stiffness, and photophobia. His left tympanic membrane is perforated with mucopurulent discharge. CT brain reveals a left temporal lobe hypodense area with ring enhancement and surrounding oedema. What is the most likely intracranial complication of his otitis media?
options: Extradural (epidural) abscess
Subdural empyema
Otogenic brain abscess
Lateral sinus thrombosis
Otitic hydrocephalus
explanation: A ring-enhancing hypodense temporal lobe lesion in the context of ongoing otitis media and neurological deterioration (confusion, meningism) is the classic CT picture of an otogenic brain abscess. The temporal lobe is the most common site for otogenic abscesses as the middle ear is anatomically adjacent. Drowsiness and confusion indicate raised intracranial pressure from the space-occupying abscess cavity.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Extradural abscess forms between the bone and dura; it rarely produces mass-effect ring enhancement on CT and typically presents with localised headache and tenderness rather than focal temporal lobe lesion.
Subdural empyema produces a crescent-shaped extra-axial collection rather than a ring-enhancing intra-parenchymal lesion and tends to spread rapidly over the cortical surface.
This is the correct answer — otogenic brain abscess in the temporal lobe is demonstrated by ring-enhancing parenchymal lesion with surrounding oedema following middle ear infection.
Lateral sinus thrombosis presents with spiking fever, septicaemia, and raised ICP but does not produce an intra-parenchymal ring-enhancing lesion on CT.
Otitic hydrocephalus (benign intracranial hypertension secondary to OM) presents with headache and papilloedema but normal or near-normal CT without a focal ring-enhancing lesion.
clinicalPearl: Temporal lobe ring-enhancing lesion + otitis media = otogenic brain abscess until proven otherwise; urgent neurosurgical referral is mandatory.
keyClue: Ring-enhancing temporal lobe lesion on CT following prolonged otitis media with systemic sepsis and confusion.
commonExamTrap: Candidates often choose lateral sinus thrombosis because of the septic picture, but the CT ring-enhancing parenchymal lesion specifically points to brain abscess.
missedConcept: The anatomical proximity of the temporal lobe to the middle ear and the CT hallmark of brain abscess (ring enhancement, surrounding oedema).
redFlagReason: Otogenic brain abscess is a life-threatening complication causing raised ICP, herniation, and death if not urgently drained; altered consciousness demands immediate neurosurgical and ID input.

clinicalStem: A 38-year-old woman underwent canal-wall-up mastoidectomy for acquired cholesteatoma of the left ear 18 months ago. She reports no pain or discharge and feels well. Her audiogram shows a mild conductive hearing loss of 25 dB. On examination the canal is clean and the post-auricular scar is well healed. What is the most important reason for her planned second-look operation at 12–18 months post-surgery?
options: To harvest ossicular grafts for ossiculoplasty
To detect and remove residual or recurrent cholesteatoma
To revise the mastoid cavity and reduce the risk of otitis externa
To assess sensorineural hearing function after surgery
To inspect for development of a perilymph fistula
explanation: Canal-wall-up (closed) mastoidectomy preserves the posterior canal wall and has excellent cosmetic and audiological outcomes, but at the cost of a higher residual/recurrent cholesteatoma rate compared with canal-wall-down procedures. Because residual matrix can be hidden behind the intact posterior wall, a second-look operation at 12–18 months is the standard of care to identify and remove any residual keratin matrix before it causes further bony erosion.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Ossicular grafts are typically harvested and potentially implanted at the primary operation; this is not the driving indication for a planned second look.
This is correct — the second-look procedure is specifically designed to detect residual cholesteatoma hidden by the intact posterior canal wall, which carries a recurrence rate of up to 30–40%.
Mastoid cavity revision and obliteration may be performed, but this is a secondary goal; the primary reason for second look is cholesteatoma surveillance.
Sensorineural hearing assessment is done with audiometry, not surgery; a second-look operation is not indicated for audiometric evaluation alone.
Perilymph fistula is a separate entity not related to post-cholesteatoma second-look surveillance.
clinicalPearl: Canal-wall-up mastoidectomy requires a planned second-look at 12–18 months because residual cholesteatoma rates can reach 30–40%; MRI with diffusion-weighted sequences (DWI) can reduce but not yet eliminate the need for surgical re-exploration.
keyClue: Canal-wall-up mastoidectomy for cholesteatoma — planned second-look surgery at 12–18 months.
commonExamTrap: Candidates may think the second look is for hearing reconstruction; the primary purpose is cholesteatoma surveillance, not audiological improvement.
missedConcept: The fundamental trade-off of canal-wall-up technique: better hearing/cosmesis versus higher residual disease rate necessitating second-look surgery.

clinicalStem: A 14-year-old girl presents with 10 days of left ear pain, fever, and progressive headache. She has been on oral antibiotics for 5 days without improvement. On examination she is febrile (39.2°C), has left postauricular tenderness with anterior displacement of the pinna, and marked tenderness over the left mastoid. She has bilateral papilloedema on fundoscopy. Her WBC is 22 × 10⁹/L. CT with contrast reveals left mastoiditis and an obliterated left sigmoid sinus with adjacent bone erosion. Which of the following is the most appropriate immediate management?
options: Oral antibiotic upgrade to co-amoxiclav and urgent ENT outpatient review
Lumbar puncture to exclude bacterial meningitis before any surgical intervention
IV broad-spectrum antibiotics, urgent cortical mastoidectomy, and anticoagulation
Intravenous dexamethasone to reduce intracranial hypertension pending imaging
Immediate myringotomy and ventilation tube insertion
explanation: Lateral (sigmoid) sinus thrombosis is a life-threatening intracranial complication of mastoiditis presenting with spiking fever, septicaemia, raised ICP (papilloedema), and mastoid signs. Management requires IV broad-spectrum antibiotics targeting mastoiditis organisms (including anaerobes), urgent cortical mastoidectomy to drain the infected focus and decompress the sinus, and therapeutic anticoagulation to prevent propagation of the thrombus. Delaying definitive surgical intervention risks propagation, septic emboli, and death.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Oral antibiotics are entirely inadequate for this life-threatening intracranial complication; outpatient management would be dangerous and inappropriate.
Lumbar puncture is relatively contraindicated with significant papilloedema due to risk of tonsillar herniation; it should not precede surgical decompression in this setting.
This is correct — the triad of IV antibiotics, mastoidectomy (source control), and anticoagulation is the standard-of-care for lateral sinus thrombosis.
Steroids alone without surgical source control are insufficient and delay definitive treatment; they may have a role adjunctively but are not the immediate priority.
Myringotomy addresses middle ear pressure only; it does not drain the mastoid focus or treat sigmoid sinus thrombosis.
clinicalPearl: Lateral sinus thrombosis = mastoiditis + spiking fever + papilloedema; treatment is IV antibiotics + mastoidectomy + anticoagulation — this is a surgical emergency.
keyClue: CT-obliterated sigmoid sinus with mastoiditis, papilloedema, and high fever unresponsive to oral antibiotics.
commonExamTrap: Performing lumbar puncture before CT/surgery in a patient with papilloedema risks fatal herniation — CT and surgical referral come first.
missedConcept: Sigmoid sinus thrombosis requires both source-control surgery and anticoagulation; antibiotics alone are insufficient.
redFlagReason: Lateral sinus thrombosis is life-threatening with risk of septicaemia, septic emboli to lungs/brain, and raised ICP causing herniation; immediate surgical and anticoagulation management is required.

topic: Benign paroxysmal positional vertigo — prognosis and recurrence
clinicalStem: A 55-year-old woman is diagnosed with right posterior canal benign paroxysmal positional vertigo (BPPV) following a Dix-Hallpike test showing right-beating upbeat-torsional nystagmus. She undergoes a successful Epley manoeuvre with complete resolution of vertigo. During her 6-week follow-up visit she asks about her long-term prognosis. Which of the following best describes the expected natural history and risk she should be counselled on?
options: BPPV is almost never self-limiting and without surgery will invariably progress to Ménière's disease
After successful Epley manoeuvre, recurrence is extremely rare (< 2% over 5 years) and no follow-up is needed
BPPV has a good prognosis; spontaneous resolution is common, but recurrence occurs in approximately 15–50% of patients within 1 year and may require repeat repositioning
Once successfully treated, BPPV permanently converts to a central vestibular disorder in one third of patients
Patients who respond to Epley manoeuvre have a 70% chance of developing unilateral sensorineural hearing loss within 5 years
explanation: BPPV has an excellent prognosis; most cases resolve with canalith repositioning and a proportion resolve spontaneously without treatment. However, recurrence rates are significant — approximately 15–50% of patients experience at least one recurrence within 12 months, and rates increase cumulatively over years. Patients should be counselled that repeat Epley manoeuvres are highly effective for recurrences and that the condition itself carries no risk of progression to central disease or permanent hearing loss.
wrongAnswerExplanations: BPPV does not progress to Ménière's disease; they are distinct pathophysiological entities with different mechanisms and prognoses.
Recurrence rates after Epley are far higher than 2%; published figures range from 15% to 50% at one year, making routine follow-up important.
This is correct — good overall prognosis with meaningful recurrence risk of 15–50% at 1 year, manageable with repeat repositioning.
BPPV does not convert to central vestibular disorders; central causes must be excluded initially, but successful peripheral BPPV treatment confirms peripheral aetiology.
BPPV is not associated with sensorineural hearing loss; it is a purely mechanical (otolithic) disorder with no cochlear involvement.
clinicalPearl: BPPV recurrence rate is 15–50% within the first year; patients should be taught a home Epley manoeuvre for early self-management of recurrences.
keyClue: Successful Epley for posterior canal BPPV — patient asking about long-term prognosis and recurrence risk.
commonExamTrap: Students confuse BPPV with Ménière's disease; BPPV causes no hearing loss and does not progress to Ménière's.
missedConcept: Despite excellent immediate response to repositioning, BPPV recurrence is common and patients require counselling and follow-up.

clinicalStem: A 46-year-old man with a 4-year history of left-sided Ménière's disease (episodic vertigo, fluctuating low-frequency sensorineural hearing loss, tinnitus, and aural fullness) presents for a follow-up appointment. He has been managed with dietary salt restriction and betahistine. His most recent audiogram shows progression of hearing loss to 55 dB HL across all frequencies in the left ear. Which of the following statements about the long-term prognosis of his condition is most accurate?
options: The vertigo attacks will almost certainly continue to worsen indefinitely, and bilateral involvement is inevitable in all patients
Hearing function in Ménière's disease typically returns fully to normal between attacks as the disease progresses
Over time, the vertigo attacks often decrease in frequency and severity ('burnt-out' phase), but permanent low-to-moderate hearing loss in the affected ear is the expected outcome
Sensorineural hearing loss in Ménière's disease affects only the high frequencies and does not progress beyond mild severity
Bilateral Ménière's disease develops in over 80% of patients within 2 years of diagnosis
explanation: In classical Ménière's disease, the long-term course is characterised by a paradox: vertigo attacks tend to diminish or cease over years as the disease 'burns out' (thought to relate to end-stage hydropic changes), but irreversible sensorineural hearing loss accumulates in the affected ear. By the time vestibular function is lost, most patients have a permanent moderate-to-severe hearing loss. Bilateral involvement occurs in approximately 15–40% over a lifetime, not universally or within 2 years.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Vertigo does not inexorably worsen indefinitely; the natural history often includes spontaneous reduction in attack frequency ('burnt-out' Ménière's), though at the cost of hearing.

Hearing fluctuates early in Ménière's, but with disease progression residual hearing loss accumulates and inter-attack recovery becomes incomplete; full normalisation does not occur in advanced disease.
This is the correct answer — eventual reduction of vertigo attacks alongside progressive permanent hearing loss is the characteristic long-term trajectory.
Early Ménière's preferentially affects low frequencies; progression leads to pan-frequency sensorineural loss that can reach moderate-to-severe levels.
Bilateral involvement over a lifetime is approximately 15–40%, not 80%; and the time course is years to decades, not 2 years.
clinicalPearl: Ménière's disease often 'burns out' with age — vertigo attacks diminish, but at the cost of permanent sensorineural hearing loss; counsel patients on realistic hearing prognosis.
keyClue: 4-year Ménière's history with progressive pan-frequency hearing loss to 55 dB — asking about long-term prognosis.
commonExamTrap: Students assume ongoing vertigo indefinitely; the burnt-out phase is an important prognostic concept they often miss.
missedConcept: The natural history of Ménière's involves eventual spontaneous reduction in vertigo but irreversible progressive sensorineural hearing loss.

topic: Noise-induced hearing loss — prognosis and prevention
clinicalStem: A 32-year-old male factory worker presents after a pre-employment audiogram reveals a bilateral high-frequency sensorineural hearing loss with a characteristic notch at 4 kHz, worse on the right. He denies tinnitus at rest but reports ringing after each working shift. He has been working in the factory without ear protection for 6 years. He asks whether his hearing will improve if he leaves the job. Which is the most accurate statement to give him?
options: Noise-induced sensorineural hearing loss is fully reversible if noise exposure is stopped within 10 years
The 4 kHz notch is a temporary threshold shift and will resolve completely within 2 weeks of noise cessation
Established noise-induced sensorineural hearing loss is permanent and irreversible; removing noise exposure prevents further deterioration but does not restore lost hearing
Hearing aids are contraindicated in noise-induced hearing loss as they risk further hair cell damage
Noise-induced hearing loss primarily affects the low frequencies and characteristically spares the speech frequencies
explanation: Chronic noise-induced hearing loss results from irreversible destruction of cochlear outer hair cells, particularly those tuned to 4 kHz. Once established as a permanent threshold shift (as opposed to temporary threshold shift which recovers within hours to days), the damage cannot be reversed. Removing noise exposure is critical to prevent further progression but will not restore already-lost function. Early detection, hearing protection, and audiometric monitoring are the pillars of management.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Sensorineural hearing loss from chronic noise exposure is not reversible regardless of duration; outer hair cell destruction is permanent.
A persistent 4 kHz notch after years of exposure represents permanent threshold shift, not temporary threshold shift; TTS resolves within 16–48 hours of noise cessation.
This is the correct answer — established NIHL is permanent; cessation of exposure limits further damage but cannot restore hearing.
Hearing aids are appropriate rehabilitation tools for NIHL; they do not cause additional cochlear damage at properly fitted amplification levels.
NIHL classically affects high frequencies, with the 4 kHz notch being pathognomonic; low frequencies and speech frequencies (500–2000 Hz) are relatively spared early in the disease.
clinicalPearl: The 4 kHz audiometric notch is pathognomonic of noise-induced hearing loss; it is permanent — prevention and hearing protection before damage occurs is the only truly effective intervention.
keyClue: Bilateral 4 kHz audiometric notch after years of occupational noise exposure without hearing protection.
commonExamTrap: Confusing temporary threshold shift (reversible, resolves in hours–days) with permanent threshold shift (irreversible hair cell death).
missedConcept: Permanent sensorineural hearing loss from noise cannot be reversed; the importance of primary prevention (ear protection) cannot be overstated.

topic: Malignant (necrotising) otitis externa — prognosis and complications
clinicalStem: A 72-year-old man with poorly controlled type 2 diabetes presents with a 3-week history of severe, relentless left otalgia disproportionate to the clinical findings. On examination there is granulation tissue at the bony-cartilaginous junction of the left external auditory canal. His WBC is 13.5 × 10⁹/L, ESR 95 mm/hr, and CRP 120 mg/L. Temporal bone CT reveals cortical erosion of the temporal bone extending to the skull base. Pseudomonas aeruginosa is cultured from the canal. Which complication would most directly explain a new onset of left facial weakness (House-Brackmann grade IV) in this patient?
options: Direct invasion of the middle ear ossicles causing conductive hearing loss and referred facial nerve palsy
Spread of Pseudomonas infection along the skull base eroding the stylomastoid foramen and facial nerve canal
Diabetic peripheral neuropathy coincidentally affecting the facial nerve
Iatrogenic facial nerve injury from aural toilet instrumentation
Labyrinthitis with secondary facial nerve oedema from serous inflammation
explanation: Malignant otitis externa (MOE) is an aggressive osteomyelitis of the skull base in immunocompromised (typically diabetic or elderly) patients caused predominantly by Pseudomonas aeruginosa. As the infection erodes along the skull base, it can involve cranial nerve foramina — most commonly the stylomastoid foramen (CN VII), followed by the jugular foramen (CN IX, X, XI). Facial nerve palsy in MOE carries a poor prognosis and indicates advanced skull base invasion.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Ossicular involvement causes conductive hearing loss; it does not directly cause facial nerve palsy, which requires nerve canal or stylomastoid foramen involvement.
This is correct — skull base erosion reaching the stylomastoid foramen and facial nerve canal by Pseudomonas osteomyelitis is the mechanism of CN VII palsy in malignant OE.
Diabetic peripheral neuropathy is diffuse and does not cause acute unilateral facial nerve palsy; this is a coincidental distractor.
Aural toilet is a superficial procedure; it does not reach the facial nerve which lies deep to the tympanic membrane.
Labyrinthitis may cause sensorineural hearing loss and vertigo, not typically facial nerve palsy of House-Brackmann grade IV.
clinicalPearl: Facial nerve palsy in malignant otitis externa indicates skull base osteomyelitis at the stylomastoid foramen — a prognostic red flag requiring prolonged IV anti-pseudomonal therapy (6–8 weeks).
keyClue: Diabetic elderly man, granulation tissue at bony-cartilaginous junction, skull base erosion on CT, Pseudomonas culture, new facial palsy.
commonExamTrap: Candidates assume facial palsy in a diabetic is diabetic neuropathy; in the context of MOE it is a specific complication of skull base erosion.
missedConcept: MOE progresses from the external canal to skull base; facial nerve involvement at the stylomastoid foramen is the most common cranial nerve complication and worsens prognosis.
redFlagReason: Skull base osteomyelitis with facial nerve palsy represents advanced infection that can progress to involve multiple cranial nerves, meningitis, and death; requires prolonged IV anti-pseudomonal therapy and urgent specialist management.

clinicalStem: A 38-year-old woman underwent right stapedectomy for otosclerosis 3 weeks ago. She had initial good hearing improvement. She now re-presents urgently with sudden onset of severe right-sided sensorineural hearing loss, roaring tinnitus, and episodic vertigo that started after she strained during heavy lifting yesterday. Otoscopy reveals an intact tympanic membrane. Audiometry confirms a new profound sensorineural hearing loss in the right ear. What is the most likely diagnosis explaining this acute deterioration?
options: Recurrent otosclerosis with re-fixation of the prosthesis
Perilymph fistula at the oval window with labyrinthine hydrops
Otogenic meningitis from post-operative infection
Displacement of the stapes prosthesis into the vestibule
Eustachian tube dysfunction with retraction of the tympanic membrane
explanation: Sudden profound sensorineural hearing loss with vertigo following straining (Valsalva) shortly after stapedectomy is the classic presentation of stapes prosthesis displacement into the vestibule. Increased intralabyrinthine pressure during straining can drive a recently placed prosthesis deeper into the vestibule, causing direct mechanical trauma to the membranous labyrinth. This is a true surgical emergency requiring urgent return to theatre for prosthesis repositioning or removal to prevent permanent cochlear damage.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Recurrent otosclerosis (re-fixation) occurs months to years post-operatively as a slowly progressive conductive loss, not sudden sensorineural deterioration after straining.
Perilymph fistula causes fluctuating hearing loss and positional vertigo but rarely produces profound sensorineural loss acutely; displacement is the more dangerous and more acute diagnosis here.
Post-operative infection presents with fever, pain, and discharge over days; it does not cause instantaneous profound sensorineural loss following straining.
This is correct — prosthesis displacement into the vestibule after straining is the most dangerous post-stapedectomy complication, requiring urgent surgical re-exploration.
Eustachian tube dysfunction produces conductive hearing loss and tympanic membrane changes, not sudden profound sensorineural hearing loss.
clinicalPearl: Sudden profound sensorineural hearing loss + vertigo after Valsalva/straining in the early post-stapedectomy period = prosthesis displacement into vestibule — a surgical emergency.
keyClue: Profound sensorineural hearing loss and vertigo immediately following straining 3 weeks after stapedectomy.
commonExamTrap: Confusing this with perilymph fistula; displacement is more profound, more acute, and directly caused by trauma from straining post-operatively.
missedConcept: Patients must avoid Valsalva manoeuvres and heavy lifting post-stapedectomy; prosthesis displacement is the most serious early surgical complication.
redFlagReason: Prosthesis displacement into the vestibule causes direct labyrinthine trauma and permanent sensorineural hearing loss if not urgently re-explored; time-critical surgical emergency.

topic: Vestibular neuritis — prognosis and chronic dizziness
clinicalStem: A 42-year-old nurse had a severe episode of vestibular neuritis 4 months ago that left her unable to walk for 2 days. The acute vertigo resolved and her caloric testing showed a 60% canal paresis on the right. She now reports chronic daily unsteadiness and a rocking sensation that is worse in supermarkets and when using a computer. She has no recurrent episodic vertigo, her Romberg test is mildly positive, and head impulse test remains abnormal to the right. She has not started vestibular rehabilitation. What is the most important prognostic intervention to recommend at this stage?
options: Prescription of long-term cinnarizine to suppress the residual vestibular imbalance
Referral for surgical labyrinthectomy on the right to eliminate the asymmetric vestibular input
Structured vestibular rehabilitation physiotherapy to promote central compensation
Repeat MRI brain to exclude a central lesion causing her persistent symptoms
Oral corticosteroids for 3 weeks to treat residual inflammation of the vestibular nerve
explanation: After acute vestibular neuritis, central compensation normally occurs over weeks to months; however, this process requires movement and sensory challenges to drive neuroplastic adaptation in the cerebellum. Chronic vestibular suppressants (e.g., cinnarizine, prochlorperazine) are appropriate for acute phase only — prolonged use actually inhibits central compensation. Vestibular rehabilitation exercises (Cawthorne-Cooksey, Brandt-Daroff, gaze stabilisation) are the evidence-based treatment to promote compensation and reduce chronic postural instability.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Long-term vestibular suppressants actively impair central compensation; after the acute phase they should be discontinued and rehabilitation commenced.
Labyrinthectomy is a last-resort intervention for intractable Ménière's disease or vestibular schwannoma, not for post-neuritis compensation failure in a patient who has not yet tried rehabilitation.
This is correct — vestibular rehabilitation is the cornerstone treatment to drive central compensation and resolve chronic unsteadiness after vestibular neuritis.

MRI may be appropriate if atypical features suggest a central cause, but normal head impulse test (which is abnormal here, as expected peripherally) and typical history do not warrant repeat MRI before trialling rehabilitation.
Corticosteroids have a role in the acute phase of vestibular neuritis (within 1–2 weeks of onset); at 4 months, anti-inflammatory therapy is not indicated and will not improve prognosis.
clinicalPearl: Prolonged use of vestibular suppressants after the acute phase of vestibular neuritis delays central compensation; vestibular rehabilitation is the key to recovery at the chronic stage.
keyClue: Chronic unsteadiness 4 months after vestibular neuritis, no rehabilitation yet commenced, still on vestibular suppressants.
commonExamTrap: Prescribing long-term cinnarizine or prochlorperazine — this common clinical error actually worsens long-term outcome by suppressing compensatory neuroplasticity.
missedConcept: Central vestibular compensation requires movement challenges; vestibular rehabilitation must be started and vestibular suppressants weaned after the acute phase.

topic: Sudden sensorineural hearing loss — prognosis and follow-up
clinicalStem: A 45-year-old woman wakes up with sudden right-sided hearing loss. She describes waking and immediately noticing silence on the right, with high-pitched tinnitus. There is no preceding trauma, ototoxic medication use, or infection. Otoscopy is normal. Urgent audiometry reveals a 50 dB unilateral right sensorineural hearing loss across all frequencies. Neurological examination is normal. What is the most important initial management step to optimise her prognosis?
options: Prescribe a 7-day course of amoxicillin for presumed viral otitis media
Order an MRI brain and defer treatment until imaging rules out acoustic neuroma
Commence high-dose oral corticosteroids (prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day) urgently within the 2-week treatment window
Refer for urgent audiological hearing aid fitting as hearing loss is likely permanent
Arrange outpatient ENT review in 4–6 weeks for repeat audiometry
explanation: Idiopathic sudden sensorineural hearing loss (ISSNHL) is defined as ≥30 dB sensorineural loss over ≥3 consecutive frequencies developing within 72 hours. High-dose systemic corticosteroids are the only evidence-based treatment that improves prognosis; they should be commenced urgently within 2 weeks of onset (ideally within 24–48 hours). Delayed treatment beyond 2 weeks significantly reduces the chance of recovery. MRI is important for long-term workup (to exclude vestibular schwannoma) but must not delay steroid initiation.
wrongAnswerExplanations: There are no features of otitis media; ISSNHL is sensorineural (not conductive) and does not respond to antibiotics.
MRI is essential in follow-up to exclude vestibular schwannoma (found in 2–4% of ISSNHL), but imaging must not delay urgent steroid treatment — the treatment window is time-critical.
This is correct — high-dose oral prednisolone commenced urgently within the treatment window is the cornerstone of ISSNHL management to maximise hearing recovery.
Hearing aid fitting is premature at this stage; prognosis with steroids is reasonable and permanent loss should not be assumed before treatment is attempted.
A 4–6 week outpatient review without treatment would be negligent; the treatment window closes and the chance of recovery is lost.
clinicalPearl: Sudden SNHL is an audiological emergency — high-dose steroids must be commenced within 24–48 hours of onset (and not later than 2 weeks); do not delay for MRI.
keyClue: Sudden unilateral sensorineural hearing loss on waking — time-critical treatment window for steroids.
commonExamTrap: Ordering MRI before starting steroids — imaging is important for workup but must not delay time-critical corticosteroid therapy.
missedConcept: ISSNHL is an audiological emergency with a narrow treatment window; steroid delay beyond 2 weeks significantly worsens prognosis.

topic: Auricular haematoma — complications of untreated haematoma
clinicalStem: A 24-year-old rugby player is seen in the emergency department 3 hours after a tackle. His right auricle is tender, swollen, and there is a tense, fluctuant, non-pulsatile swelling over the anterior surface of the upper helix, with loss of the normal auricular contour. The skin overlying the swelling is intact and not erythematous. He is otherwise well. What is the most important complication if this condition is not promptly treated?
options: Auricular cellulitis spreading to the orbit causing cavernous sinus thrombosis
Avascular necrosis of the auricular cartilage leading to permanent deformity (cauliflower ear)
Acute septicaemia from haematogenous spread of cartilage organisms
Sensorineural hearing loss from haematoma pressure on the external auditory meatus
Spontaneous resorption of the haematoma within 72 hours without sequelae
explanation: Auricular haematoma collects between the perichondrium and the cartilage. Auricular cartilage is avascular and relies entirely on the perichondrium for its nutrient supply. An untreated haematoma strips the perichondrium from the cartilage, causing ischaemic necrosis. The resulting cartilage dissolution and fibrosis produces the classic 'cauliflower ear' deformity. Treatment requires prompt aspiration or incision and drainage with a compression dressing to re-appose perichondrium to cartilage and restore nutrient supply.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Cellulitis and cavernous sinus thrombosis are possible with auricular infection (perichondritis/chondritis), but the primary and most common complication of untreated haematoma specifically is cartilage necrosis and cauliflower ear.
This is correct — perichondrial stripping by haematoma causes avascular necrosis of auricular cartilage, leading to permanent cauliflower ear deformity.
Septicaemia from an acute non-infected haematoma is not a primary complication; infection may follow if drainage is contaminated, but it is not the key initial risk.
Haematoma of the auricle does not compress the external auditory meatus sufficiently to affect hearing; hearing loss is not a recognised complication.
Spontaneous resorption does not occur reliably; untreated haematomas organise and fibrose, causing permanent deformity — they do not simply resorb without sequelae.
clinicalPearl: Auricular cartilage is avascular — auricular haematoma must be drained urgently and a compression dressing applied to prevent avascular necrosis and cauliflower ear deformity.
keyClue: Tense fluctuant swelling of the auricle after trauma with loss of normal contour — perichondrial stripping from cartilage.
commonExamTrap: Assuming haematomas self-resolve; auricular haematomas must be actively drained as spontaneous resorption leads to permanent deformity.
missedConcept: Auricular cartilage depends solely on perichondrium for nutrition; haematoma between them causes ischaemic cartilage necrosis if not urgently drained.

clinicalStem: A 35-year-old woman with chronic suppurative otitis media (tubotympanic type) underwent left tympanoplasty using a temporalis fascia underlay graft 8 weeks ago. At her follow-up visit, she reports intermittent water entry sensation in the ear after showering. Otoscopy reveals a small anterior residual perforation in the neo-tympanic membrane with an otherwise intact graft. Her Eustachian tube function is clinically poor. Pre-operatively she had bilateral disease with a contralateral smaller perforation. Which of the following is the most important prognostic factor determining long-term success of her tympanoplasty?
options: The surgical technique used (underlay vs overlay fascia graft)
The size of the original perforation before surgery
Eustachian tube function and middle ear ventilation
The patient's age and sex
The duration of pre-operative otorrhoea
explanation: Eustachian tube function is the single most critical determinant of tympanoplasty success. The neo-tympanic membrane requires adequate middle ear ventilation to remain viable; poor Eustachian tube function leads to negative middle ear pressure, chronic effusion, and progressive retraction or re-perforation of the graft. Patients with persistently dysfunctional Eustachian tubes have significantly higher rates of graft failure regardless of surgical technique or perforation size.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Underlay vs overlay technique influences graft take rate in specific anatomical situations but is not the most powerful independent prognostic factor overall.
Perforation size influences technical difficulty and may affect take rate, but is not the dominant long-term prognostic determinant — Eustachian tube function supersedes it.
This is correct — Eustachian tube function determines whether the middle ear can maintain normal pressure and ventilation; dysfunction is the primary cause of long-term graft failure.
Age and sex have minor effects; Eustachian tube function is a far stronger predictor than demographic factors.
Duration of otorrhoea may reflect chronicity but is not an independent predictor of graft survival; functional tube status is more important.
clinicalPearl: Eustachian tube dysfunction is the primary cause of tympanoplasty failure and re-perforation; always assess and document tube function pre-operatively and counsel patients accordingly.
keyClue: Post-tympanoplasty residual perforation with clinically poor Eustachian tube function — asking for dominant prognostic factor.
commonExamTrap: Students focus on graft size or technique; Eustachian tube function is the most important independent prognostic variable for long-term success.
missedConcept: A structurally successful graft still fails if the Eustachian tube cannot maintain adequate middle ear ventilation; tube function assessment is mandatory pre-operatively.
